old_claim.note: Alchemy provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Jabirian and al-Razi alchemical writings used the same distillation methods and apparatus as al-Kindi's perfume-making practice, making the medieval Arabic alchemical laboratory tradition a direct experimental enabler without making transmutation theory required.

source_shape.source_locator: pp. 80-81 and 89-90 compare al-Kindi's perfume-distillation procedure with Jabirian and al-Razi alchemical methods and identify shared water-bath and direct-fire methodology.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Practical perfume making and alchemical distillation were conceptually distinct but often used the same medieval apparatus and heating methods.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The edge remains enabling because the Arabic alchemical laboratory tradition supplied a directly shared experimental method, while the node scope does not require belief in transmutation.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-medieval-alembic-distillation-scope: alchemy remains an enabling edge rather than a required theoretical commitment.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed solely to al-Kindi's anti-alchemical perfume recipes with no shared apparatus lineage.
A stronger source shows the Jabirian and al-Razi procedures were technically unrelated to the scoped alembic practice.
A more precise medieval_laboratory_apparatus node is added and should replace the broad alchemy edge.
short_reason: Shared apparatus and heating practice make Arabic alchemy an evidenced enabler, not a required theory.
